mūla.devanāgarī: एवं ज्ञात्वा कृतं कर्म पूर्वैर् अपि मुमुक्षुभिः | कुरु कर्मैव तस्मात् त्वं पूर्वैः पूर्वतरं कृतम्
mūla.iast: evaṃ jñātvā kṛtaṃ karma pūrvair api mumukṣubhiḥ | kuru karmaiva tasmāt tvaṃ pūrvaiḥ pūrvataraṃ kṛtam
mūla.chapter_position: Chapter 4 (Jñāna-Karma-Sannyāsa-Yoga (The Yoga of Renunciation of Action in Knowledge)), verse 15
mūla.speaker: Krishna

senses_attested_in_panel.sense: पूर्वैरपि मुमुक्षुभिः कक्षीवन्नारदादिभिर्मन्वादिभिर्वा जनकादिभिर्वा कर्म स्वधर्माख्यं वक्ष्यमाणप्रकारेण कृतम् तस्मात्त्
पूर्वैर्जनकादिभिरपि मुमुक्षुभिः सत्त्वशुद्ध्यर्थं पूर्वतरं युगान्तरेष्वपि कृतम्
कृतं कर्म पूर्वैरतिक्रान्तैरपि अस्मिन् युगे ययातियदुप्रभृतिभिर्मुमुक्षुभिः
। तस्मात् त्वम् उक्तप्रकारमद्विषयज्ञानविधूतपापः पूर्वैः विवस्वन्मन्वादिभिः कृतं पूर्वतरं पुरातनं तदानीम् एव मया उक्तं वक
। एतेनास्मिन्युगेऽन्ययुगे च पूर्वपूर्वतरैः कृतत्वादवश्यं त्वया कर्तव्यं कर्मेति दर्शयति।
कृतम्। तस्मात् त्वम् उक्तप्रकारमद्विषयज्ञानविधूतपापः पूर्वैः विवस्वन्मन्वादिभिः कृतं पूर्वतरं पुरातनं तदानीम् एव मया उक
तेन कर्मैव त्वम् न तूष्णीमासनं नापि संन्यासः कर्तव्यः तस्मात् त्वं पूर्वैरपि अनुष्ठितत्वात् यदि अनात्मज्ञः त्वं तदा आत्
। न चेदमाधुनिकम् किन्तु पूर्वतरं पूर्वैश्च कृतम्। इति शिष्टाचारात्कर्त्तव्यता बोधिता।
कृतम्। तस्मात् त्वम् उक्तप्रकारमद्विषयज्ञानविधूतपापः पूर्वैः विवस्वन्मन्वादिभिः कृतं पूर्वतरं पुरातनं तदानीम् एव मया उक
। तस्मात् त्वम् उक्तप्रकारमद्विषयज्ञानविधूतपापः पूर्वैः विवस्वन्मन्वादिभिः कृतं पूर्वतरं पुरातनं तदानीम् एव मया उक्तं वक
। एतेनास्मिन्युगेऽन्ययुगे च पूर्वपूर्वतरैः कृतत्वादवश्यं त्वया कर्तव्यं कर्मेति दर्शयति।

corpus_provenance.mūla: Belvalkar critical edition (BORI 1947), via Ambuda multi-witness

so_what_questions: Śaṅkara and Rāmānuja agree you must act but disagree about why — one says to purify the mind, the other says to serve Bhagavān. Does the motive change the quality of the action, or only its metaphysical status?

The verse appeals to precedent (pūrvair api, pūrvataram) rather than logical proof. Does the antiquity of a practice make it obligatory, or is Kṛṣṇa making a different kind of argument about lineage transmission?
All six schools rule out both passive silence (tūṣṇīmāsana) and formal renunciation (saṃnyāsa) for Arjuna here. What conditions would make renunciation appropriate — and who decides?
Vallabha names Kakṣīvat and Nārada alongside Manu and Janaka. Why does the diversity of exemplars matter? Does a sage-musician (Nārada) performing karma carry the same authority as a king (Janaka)?
Madhusūdana adds a second temporal layer — not just prior generations but prior yugas. Does the multi-yuga span change what is at stake, or is it rhetorical amplification?
Madhva is conspicuously brief here. What does the silence of a polemical commentator on a verse emphasizing precedent and conduct suggest about which doctrinal battles this verse does not fight?

Śrīdhara specifies sattva-śuddhi (purification of the quality of luminous clarity) as the explicit goal. How does this differ from Śaṅkara's ātma-śuddhi (purification of the self-instrument) — same process, or different target?

everyday_applications.advaita: Before stepping into a meeting where you will be evaluated, remind yourself: the witness is not the presenter. Do the work thoroughly — prepare, deliver, respond — without making your identity contingent on the outcome. This is Śaṅkara's ātma-śuddhi in professional life: the ego is not purified by avoiding challenges but by passing through them without clinging.
everyday_applications.viśiṣṭādvaita: Before a significant act — a difficult conversation, a professional decision — recall the chain of practitioners in your own field who navigated the same tension before you. Rāmānuja's point is that you are not improvising alone; you are continuing an authorized lineage. This transforms anxiety into a form of kainkarya (service) extended across time.
everyday_applications.dvaita: When tempted to invent a novel approach simply because it feels original, check whether established practice (ācāra) already solves the problem. Madhva's reading of this verse is a caution against the vanity of reinvention: correct dependence on tested methods is not limitation but humility before a larger intelligence.
everyday_applications.śuddhādvaita: When routine tasks feel mechanical, reframe them as participation in a pattern larger than yourself — Vallabha's yogibhāvata mode. The task does not change; your relationship to it does. Cook the meal, file the report, answer the message as an act that joins a very long human practice of offering, not as a transaction to be completed and forgotten.
everyday_applications.bhakti: If you are early in a practice — meditation, craft, study — and feel drawn to skip the fundamentals toward more advanced work, Śrīdhara's prathama karmā is the corrective: do the foundational thing first, fully, without shortcutting. Sattva-śuddhi precedes the subtler stages; the sequence is not arbitrary.
everyday_applications.advaita-bhakti: When personal exhaustion makes action feel pointless, Madhusūdana offers two simultaneous handholds: the non-doer perspective (it is not ultimately you who acts, so the weight is not yours to carry alone) and the devotional perspective (the action is an offering, so it has a receiver). Neither alone may be sufficient in a hard moment; together they constitute a more complete support.
